unfortunately, we don't have a choice with v4 signatures. sig.signer references the [Issuer subpacket](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc4880#section-5.2.3.5) on the signature, which only contains the 8 byte keyid of the signing key.

PGPy doesn't insist on verifying self signatures because:

a) that can introduce a lot of overhead for users who may not need to re-verify their keys every time they load one up

b) at what point during a key's lifecycle would self-signature verification be introduced? It can't always be immediately after the object exists, because they won't always have one yet, like if it's being generated, etc.

along similar lines here, we can't depend on being able to verify those revocation signatures because they aren't guaranteed to be self-signatures, and a key has no way to obtain a reference to another key (the revoker's key may not even be anywhere in memory). So it's probably more universally useful to provide a set of candidates and let the consumer sort out verifying them if needed, as long as doing so is convenient enough.

It's a common pattern we use in a lot of places that makes it possible to change the default later on.  Imagine you have `alpha=1` here, and later decide you want `alpha=0.5` as the default.  When you then get "alpha=1", you never know if the user went for the default value or for 1.  With ``None``, that is explicit, so you can do the check ``if alpha is None: warn the user that the default has changed``.

yeah, that "pass" thing is simply a way to trick the Travis dpl tool to run `python setup.py pass` and in fact _not_ do anything. The default is that it would do `python setup.py sdist bdist_wheel`. But if you have already built your distribution packages (e.g. for C extension modules we want to compile them inside a manylinyux1 docker container for greater portability), we don't want Travis to attempt to rebuilt them. If we `skip_cleanup` and place the sdist and wheel packages inside the default `dist/` folder, then the Travis dpl tool will find them and upload those instead of rebuilding them again as part of the deploy stage.

Sounds good! This is in line with what we already to with bucketing:
dataIter.provide_data should use the default/largest bucket key so that parameter and memory sharing for different graphs is working properly. Every batch however, has its own bucket_key member that determines which graph the batch should be routed to.

In your case, I would assume that MXNet requires to know the maximum (batch) size once for each graph, allocates the respective memory, and then dynamically reshapes the graph based on the batch size of each batch, reusing already allocated memory.

Regarding bucketing, I think @tdomhan did an investigation once that if one doesn't use the right default bucket key when creating the module, MXNet does not complain but just silently does the wrong thing :)
We should make sure nothing like this can happen here, either by some empirical evidence that this is doing the right thing or by looking at the module code and try to understand the logic. On a related note: does this affect speed at all?

My reason being that while we currently have `Special Permission` as a catch all for anything not covered by the other licenses, it is possible that we could introduce new licenses with descriptions that are not `Special Permissions` - as such, I would still like them to make it into the database, rather than throw an error simply because we don't recognize the license.

In the frontend, we will display translated license names and descriptions for recognized licenses, but otherwise just show the DB value for both.

When this frontend is in place, we can also update the import code, so as not to import the description if it is for one of these recognized licenses.

ok, it appeas that neither ElementTree nor lxml.etree distinguish between these two cases when parsing, they are always parsed as `Element.text == None`. Compare: ```python >>> import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET >>> assert ET.fromstring('').text is None >>> assert ET.fromstring('').text is None >>> from lxml import etree >>> assert etree.fromstring('').text is None >>> assert etree.fromstring('').text is None ``` In the case of ufonormalizer's XMLWriter, having a value being None however, means completely dropping the element, which produces an invalid plist, as Nikolaus noted. So I think we are fine if we treat an element's text being None as an empty string, so we can write it back, like this PR is doing.

I really think the best default is for the common extensions (`.md` and `.markdown`) to get the special treatment.  While this is a bit more of an edge case that replacing tabs with spaces in a Makefile, I still think it is better to come down on the side of safety (i.e. no change to semantics) even if it means a few non-markdown `.md` files might have extraneous whitespace left in them, since at the end of the day that is a purely aesthetic consideration.  I don't expect many (any?) people to actually set these options, so the default settings are for me the most important.

Having a non-empty default requires a `store` action to clear the set.  I used an `append` action to support somebody who adds extensions one at a time, but my original code was not consistent as the first option overwrote the defaults rather than appending to them; that is too much magic. It is simpler, clearer, and safer to always append, so I made [that change instead](https://github.com/dupuy/pre-commit-hooks/commit/cc29df5d4544955713f58f5b71fc895ec7d7ca73).

The other reason for a separate `--no-markdown-linebreak-ext` option with a `store` action (rather than using a single option with a `store` action to set (or if empty, clear) the extension list) is that argparse will silently "eat" a following non-option argument (interpreting a staged filename as an extension) if you omit the `=` (e.g. `--markdown-linebreak-ext` instead of `--markdown-linebreak-ext=`).  I actually ran into that during testing, thus the `--` argument and comment in `test_markdown_linebreak_ext_opt_all`.  It's better to have the code error out if the argument provided is missing (`--` or other following option), empty (`=` or explicit empty string argument), or has a non-initial `.` (probably an innocent filename) so I [made that change too](https://github.com/dupuy/pre-commit-hooks/commit/00eb809a2518b2a2e1c99f951a1d86b97c9c2239).

Probably the most common case for the special `*` extension would be to enable linebreak preservation for files without extensions (you could use `--markdown-linebreak-ext=,` but that's pretty obscure, and I doubt anybody could figure out what that does/is supposed to do without looking at the code).

Finally, in your example you wrote `--markdown-trailing-whitespace-extension`.
I feel like the `--markdown-linebreak-ext` is already too long, but if you think the name should be changed, I'm happy to do so.

And now I think I see what you were getting at. Isn't this not really safe? You're basically skipping serialization for everything that isn't a dict, but I think what you were actually trying to get is that serialization works as a sort of pass-through restrict to `str` for py2 (assuming you can dump as simple bytes) and `bytes` for py3 for the keys. These are the only translations that make sense since everything else requires *some* sort of serialization which Avro provides (even simple strings, which I would agree should 99.999% of the time be serialized as UTF-8, but that shouldn't be absolutely assumed).

This is actually pretty tricky. Avro also provides serialization for pure bytes where that is the entirety of the schema. In some cases it is useful to be able to wrap those raw bytes. The Confluent serializers do assume that if you pass bytes, you're going to be passing raw bytes for everything and omits the framing. So we could do that for a compatible implementation, but we'd want to make sure the passthrough *only* works for str/bytes. Also, this isn't handled differently between keys and values, so the implementations should really be consistent. (Again this ties back to the fact that the current implementation is only supporting a subset of types for serialization.)

(Note, also, that this comes at a cost. It makes it impossible to mix and match types within the same topic because once you drop raw bytes in one message without any framing, there's no way to distinguish the different schemas.)

By the way, if you're thinking about making primitive types work more generally (i.e. more than just bytes, even if just utf-8 strings), I think there are basically 2 ways of making this happen. The first would be to adjust the way AvroProducer/Consumer are implemented, instead providing a SerializingProducer/Consumer and passing in the desired serializers. This lets you control the key and value serialization formats independently. Actually, looking at this now @criccomini, it seems like that might be a better path for you folks given requests re: the REST proxy? The other would be to allow a "primitive" mode in this class that knows how to serialize primitive types outside of Avro, handling the trivial types like string (utf-8), int (32-bit network order int encoding, although that gets tricker with the unification of int/long in py3), and long (64-bit network order int).

I'm probably more in favor of the former since the latter would just result in multiple implementations (albeit trivial) across different XProducers for "X" serialization format. It also gives you an easy way to provide one set of documentation for everyone with the understanding that they just need to understand the serialization library's type support for complex types. Opinions?

NumPy has a .base flag, so I thought it would be nice to expose it in the C++ API as well.

This feature (pointer into memory owned by a "base") is really neat for exposing a NumPy "view" into a data structure that may be implemented in a completely different programming language. The base pointer lets you get back from the numpy view to the parent class, which can be useful in a function that is called without knowing the latter.

I suspect it'll end up being a bit nicer if we make the stream `__init__` methods accept arbitrary pipes, and set them to non-blocking mode at that point. Then when we only want to use one side of a pipe with trio, we can write

```python
r_raw, w_raw = os.pipe()
r_trio = PipeReceiveStream(r_raw)
# ... now use r_trio and w_raw ...
```

Also, elsewhere in trio where we return a pair like this, we do `(sender, receiver)`. Which yeah is the opposite of `os.pipe`. But trio's way is easier to remember ("data flows from left to right, like you read").

Neither of these are big issues right now since we're keeping this private, and once we start trying to use it for real we'll probably find other things we want to tweak. But figured I'd mention.
